title: A Jimbo Epic Art and Sexuality Kaworu and Shinji
display_title: A Jimbo Epic Art and Sexuality Kaworu and Shinji
wikitext:
[]: A Jimbo Epic: Art & Sexuality, Kaworu & Shinji             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Oct 10, 2010 1:24 am Note: I wrote this originally intending to post it in THIS thread, but since this grew to be such a large essay, I decided to give it its own thread. So any comments referring to “this thread” is discussing the one I linked to. Hopefully the mods will let this stay, even though it's ripe for a ton of possible divergent discussions. Golly, I truly love this thread/topic/subject with a passion. You know why? Because it really brings together all of the things I love about NGE; interpretational ambiguity, audience bias, perspective, and desire, a complexity of interrelated subjects, and how all of this relates through a lil’ ol’ work of art (or “object of human creation”, if someone wants to take offense to the word “art”). What follows is a typical Jimbo text bomb in which I try to illuminate the multiple subjects that this topic concerns and how they relate to each other, while, at the same time, providing my own interpretative opinions. I’ll have to break this down into two different, but equally important and interrelated topics: Art and Sexuality. Yes, they’re both large enough topics that any full discourse could entail a book-length study on their own (much less together), so I’ll try to simplify and tailor them to the concerns of this thread. Part 1. Art: Desire, Intention, Creation / Semiotics - Ontology / Desire, Observation, Interpretation I start with this because it’s usually best to start from the beginning. It primarily concerns the argument that begins HERE , but can also be observed throughout most of Synapsid’s replies to various posters on this subject, and I especially find this comment indicative of the issue: Synapsid wrote: there isn’t some sort of false choice between the two, they aren’t unconnected things, and it isn’t a zero sum game between them, recognizing the scripts doesn’t invalidate the aired version; as Anno said they’re supposed to be the same thing.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Sun Oct 10, 2010 4:27 am Cool story bro

[]: Re: A Jimbo Epic: Art & Sexuality, Kaworu & Shinji             Quote Post by NemZ » Sun Oct 10, 2010 5:20 am tl/dr: Death of the author, hell yeah Sex and gender aren't simple Reply hazy, ask again later

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Sun Oct 10, 2010 8:35 am First off, props on the text wall -- I'm glad that someone is getting back to substantial analyses again in my self-imposed absence around here. Just popping in to throw the figurative wrench into things. This goes back to what I’ve often said about NGE being more like jazz than classical, a form of animated, narrative improvisation. Anno himself has said he wasn’t sure where the series was going, showing that he certainly had a limited conscious comprehension of his full intent.

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Sun Oct 10, 2010 10:09 am Source of Jimbo's post: http://www.elsewhere.org/pomo/

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Oct 10, 2010 10:17 am Thanks for not writing "bi-sexual", Jimbo. That's a huge pet-peeve of mine. Awesome analysis you have here. I agree with your thoughts on Kaworu and Shinji. I've always been partial to the EGF idea (from 2008 or so?) that Kaworu (and Rei) represent the ideal [lover/etc] and Instrumentality, while Asuka represents the real [lover/etc] and real world/reality. And while true that people will have their own opinions on creative works, the author's own input is important to gain a fuller understanding...

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Sun Oct 10, 2010 10:45 am Well Jimbo, if nothing else, I at least see now why you don't dig Tarkovsky all that much. As for the rest, it read well. Semiotics in art criticism (or just art criticism in general) has become something of a non-issue for me, because I've found that dwelling on these things detracts from the overall experience or relation that the work has to offer--if, indeed, there is any relation to be found or experience to be had in the first place. As such, I can't agree with the perspective you're going from regarding what art IS or what it's supposed to do/be, but that's not anything new So I'll probably just sit this out. I hafta say though, Allemann did give me a really good laugh.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Sun Oct 10, 2010 10:58 am "Semiotic ontology"? Never change Jimbo, never change. ran1 wrote: I say Xard likes moe. I don't know everything about Xard, I'm just basing it off how he acts, partial knowledge of what is said and done, and of course the persona he has developed through the presentation of various symbols and images. That being said, I may be totally wrong about assuming anything about Xard, because I am not Xard.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Sun Oct 10, 2010 11:04 am Well, man, that's why there's Marxism.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Sun Oct 10, 2010 11:05 am ran1 wrote: Well, man, that's why there's Marxism.

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Sun Oct 10, 2010 11:20 am That was 100% uncut Jimbo. Frankly, I was underwhelmed because I have heard most of it before. Not that I disagree. When it comes to the art definition circle jerk, you were, and still are, the only one who made any sense to me, and this is a very astute synthesis of your views, although I can't help but feel most that just boils down to "Shit's relative, bitches." But that's a really hyperbolic dead-end reduction, and in the practice of art criticism, it's really the only thing you can do. I'd sooner fellate the barrel of a shotgun than get into that Death of the Author BS, but even as just as amateur writer, I sometimes see people see stuff in my writing that I didn't intend, but I totally see it after they mention it. Although I haven't seen this level of technical word rape from you before. Sometimes the thought crossed my mind that you were just screwing me, but I dismissed this, like most of the things I think, as excess paranoia.

[]:             Quote Post by greatmightypoo » Sun Oct 10, 2010 12:14 pm If Kaworu woud have short hair, Shinji wouldn't be attracted to him

[]:             Quote Post by LeoXiao » Sun Oct 10, 2010 3:35 pm It seems to me that Kaworu was intended to be a vehicle for Shinji's psychological development whereas his own psyche was not really explored, much less his sexuality. Also, it must be considered that Kaworu is not really quite human to begin with, so it's not exactly valid to use the same standards to judge him. All in all, I think the main thing to carry away from this essay is "Kaworu looks at humanity from an external viewpoint and in being intrigued by man's nature, could also be assumed to be curious about the different forms of sexuality." Of course, he's an Angel, so we don't know how he looks at these things (sexuality) and if we did see it from his view, we might not even consider him bicurious.

[]:             Quote Post by Lucretius » Sun Oct 10, 2010 4:57 pm Ha ha. What a story, Jimbo.

[]:             Quote Post by chee » Mon Oct 11, 2010 1:48 am Xard wrote: That said I genuinely love moe these days because my life is meaningless, directionless void - moe is good distraction from tired face of reality and helps to keep up illusions about good people living in tranquile harmony somewhere: "I just need to find them", basically. And yet the fantasy reveals its own emptiness at the same time, leading to an unsolvable paradox of genuine affection and fetishistic exploitation, cash grab and rape porn. It's fascinating paradox and keeping mind occupied with paradoxes, philosophy, film watching, eva analysis etc. are good ways to keep one from seeing the revelationary worthlessness of the whole existence.

[]: Re: A Jimbo Epic: Art & Sexuality, Kaworu & Shinji             Quote Post by Sanada Yukimura » Mon Oct 11, 2010 4:20 am I was too lazy to read it all,so i started to the part that was most interesting for me....so i really don't know how important are these parts to u theory about Shinji&Kaworu 's homosexuality..however i need to point this,that can(or not) affect ur defense to the whole essay: Eva Yojimbo wrote: This is perhaps more speculative, but it also struck me that any heterosexuality would have to equate sexuality with homosexuality to some extent, since any sexual act they found themselves in would require them being involved as a member of their own sex. Maybe this sounds sophistic, but think about it: even if I watch heterosexual porn, I’m still associating the sexual act as occurring between a member of my own as well as opposite sex.(...)

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Oct 11, 2010 6:20 am Replies to Everyone… well, almost  SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Mon Oct 11, 2010 6:58 am chee wrote: Once I fully process having read this on an Evangelion board, the sheer irony of it goes rocketing far past full circle and off into total metaphysical clusterfuck. Then time stands still and fragments into a thousand shards of nothing, and I can see faintly the seers of being, all of them, watching back with infinite eyessssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssss

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Mon Oct 11, 2010 7:03 am Xard wrote: "Semiotic ontology" is hilarious buzzword in the sense of Allemann's link because semiotics and ontology are two entirely different things (semiotics belongs to linguistics, ontology to philosophy and metaphysics). It's good example of bizarre language you make up all the time.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Mon Oct 11, 2010 7:04 am Allemann wrote: Semiotics is an independent discipline. De Saussure, the father of modern linguistics, envisioned linguistics as semiotics's subbranch, which studies a subcategory of signs, language signs.
